questions.question_text: In a North Dakota contract for deed, who holds legal title during the payment period?
distractors: The buyer holds legal title
Title is held in escrow
The county recorder holds title
questions.explanation: In a North Dakota contract for deed, the seller retains legal title until the buyer completes all required payments. The buyer holds equitable interest during the contract period.

questions.question_text: A buyer defaults on a contract for deed. What must the seller provide before declaring cancellation?
distractors: A verbal warning at the property
A court order for eviction
A certified appraisal of the property
questions.explanation: North Dakota requires the seller to provide WRITTEN NOTICE before declaring cancellation or forfeiture of a contract for deed. The buyer then has the right to reinstate by curing the default.

questions.question_text: A contract-for-deed buyer misses two payments. The seller accelerates the balance and then seeks forfeiture for nonpayment. Is this allowed?
questions.correct_answer: No, the seller cannot forfeit for unpaid accelerated amounts
distractors: Yes, acceleration triggers immediate forfeiture
Yes, if the contract includes a default clause
Only if the buyer has less than 50% equity
questions.explanation: In North Dakota, a seller CANNOT accelerate payments and then cause forfeiture for the buyer's failure to pay the accelerated amount. This protects buyers from losing their equity through aggressive acceleration tactics.

questions.question_text: After receiving a cancellation notice on a contract for deed, what right does the ND buyer have?
questions.correct_answer: The right to reinstate by curing the default
distractors: The right to an automatic 12-month extension
The right to transfer the contract to a third party
No rights once cancellation notice is sent
questions.explanation: North Dakota law gives contract-for-deed buyers the right to REINSTATE the contract by curing the default after receiving a cancellation notice from the seller.

questions.question_text: A recorded contract for deed is cancelled. Must the cancellation notice be recorded?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, if the original contract was recorded
distractors: No, cancellation is effective without recording
Only if the buyer consents to recording
Only for properties over 10 acres
questions.explanation: When a contract for deed has been recorded with the county, the notice of cancellation must also be recorded to properly clear the title record.

questions.question_text: Under HB 1190, what must a real estate wholesaler hold in North Dakota?
questions.correct_answer: A valid North Dakota real estate license
distractors: A business license from the county
A federal securities dealer permit
Only a general contractor license
questions.explanation: HB 1190 (effective August 1, 2023) requires wholesalers to hold a valid North Dakota real estate license to engage in wholesaling activities.

questions.question_text: A wholesaler assigns a purchase contract but fails to disclose that they hold only equitable interest. What can the buyer do?
distractors: Sue for double the contract price
File a criminal complaint with the sheriff
Nothing once the contract is signed
questions.explanation: If a wholesaler fails to provide the required written disclosure, the buyer may cancel the transaction before closing without penalty and receives a refund of any money paid.

questions.question_text: Which disclosure is NOT required of a North Dakota wholesaler under HB 1190?
distractors: That they hold equitable interest only
That they may not be able to convey title
That they intend to profit from the transfer
questions.explanation: Wholesalers must disclose they hold equitable interest, may not be able to convey title, and intend to profit. However, they are not required to disclose the specific dollar amount of their expected profit.

questions.question_text: HB 1125 expanded North Dakota's wholesaling requirements effective August 1, 2025. What changed?
questions.correct_answer: Coverage expanded to all real estate types
distractors: The license requirement was eliminated
Only commercial properties were added
Disclosure became optional for small deals
questions.explanation: HB 1125 (effective August 1, 2025) expanded wholesaling requirements from residential properties with fewer than 5 units to ALL real estate transactions in North Dakota.

questions.question_text: A wholesaler fails to provide required disclosures and the seller cancels. Who keeps the earnest money?
questions.correct_answer: The seller retains the earnest money
distractors: The wholesaler keeps the earnest money
The funds are split equally
The earnest money goes to NDREC
questions.explanation: When a transaction is cancelled due to a wholesaler's failure to provide required disclosures, the seller retains the earnest money while the buyer receives a refund of any money paid.

questions.question_text: Before a deed can be recorded in North Dakota, what must the county auditor certify?
questions.correct_answer: That all property taxes are paid
distractors: That the deed was prepared by an attorney
That the purchase price is at market value
That the property passed a building inspection
questions.explanation: North Dakota requires a county auditor certificate showing that property taxes are paid before a deed can be recorded with the county recorder.

questions.question_text: A deed recorded in North Dakota lacks the grantee's post office address. What is the effect?
questions.correct_answer: The deed does not meet recording requirements
distractors: The deed is still valid and recordable
The county will add the address automatically
Only the grantor's address is required
questions.explanation: North Dakota recording requirements include the grantee's post office address. A deed missing this element does not meet the statutory requirements for recording.

questions.question_text: A 2005 deed uses metes and bounds for the legal description. What additional information must it include?
questions.correct_answer: The name and address of the drafter
distractors: A survey plat signed by the county
GPS coordinates for each boundary point
Approval from the state land office
questions.explanation: Deeds and contracts recorded after January 1, 2000 that use metes and bounds descriptions must include the name and address of the person who drafted the document.

questions.question_text: Which item is required on a deed for recording in North Dakota?
distractors: The buyer's social security number
A notarized affidavit of value
The lender's loan approval letter
questions.explanation: North Dakota deeds must include a statement of full consideration, along with grantor/grantee names, grantee's post office address, and an adequate legal description for recording.

questions.question_text: Where are deeds recorded in North Dakota?
distractors: At the state capitol in Bismarck
With the NDREC main office
At the district court clerk's office
questions.explanation: Deeds in North Dakota are recorded with the county recorder's office in the county where the property is located.
